content.full_text:
Copies filed: 18700, 18702


May 12, 1981


Roy R. Sissell
Council Member
City Hall
P.O. Box 1029
Tracy, CA 95376

A-81-05-103

Re: Telephone Advice Request

Dear Councilman Sissell:

Thank you for your recent telephone call requesting our
advice regarding your situation in Tracy. As you explained
to me, the facts are as follows:

1. Approximately 6-8 weeks ago, the City Council of
Tracy voted to approve low-income home rehabilitations financed
by HUD. The basic approval of the overall project was
only involvement which the City had in this endeavor. At
that time you had no anticipation of any increase in your
business as a result of this project approval.

2. You, as sole proprietor, own and operate a plumbing
business and are a plumbing contractor in the City of Tracy.
(Sissell's Plumbing, State License #282316, engaging in
"Complete Plumbing and Heating Repairs". A copy of your
"Yellow Pages" advertisement is attached.) You also supply
various fixtures, water heaters, etc.

3. Within the last week, a general contractor who is
bidding on an addition to and remodeling of a home under the
HUD-funded project has asked you to bid, as a subcontractor,
on the plumbing portion of the work to be done. You estimate,
preliminarily, that the bid would exceed $250, in any event.

You have asked us whether you are prohibited by the
Political Reform Act (Government Code sections 81000-91014)
from bidding on this plumbing work. My telephone response
to you was that the Act does not prohibit you from so bidding.
Under the Act, any violation which occurred, if at all,
occurred when you participated in the making of the decision,
by the City, to approve the project.

Roy R. Sissell
May 12, 1981
Page Two


Government Code Section 87100 states:

"No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in any
way attempt to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason
to know he has a financial interest."

(Emphasis added.)

The Fair Political Practices Commission has adopted
regulations interpreting this section. 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18702 is particularly pertinent to your situation:

(a) The financial effect of a governmental decision
on a financial interest of a public official is material
if the decision will have a significant effect on the
business entity, real property or source of income in
question.

(b) In determining whether it is reasonably fore-
seeable that the effects of a governmental decision
will be significant within the meaning of the general
standard set forth in paragraph (a), consideration
should be given to the following:

* * *

(3) Whether, in the case of a source of
income as defined in Government Code Section 87103(c),
of two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more received
by or promised to a public official within 12
months prior to the time the decision is made:

(A) The effect of the decision will be
to directly increase or decrease the amount
of income (other than rents) to be received
by the official by one hundred dollars ($100)
or more;...

Since you are a sole proprietor, the income to your
business is income to you. Thus, if it were reasonable for
you to foresee that approving the HUD project would result
in an increase in your business of $100 or more, then you
should have disqualified yourself from participating in such
a decision. To help guide you in making such determinations
in the future, I enclose copies of an FPPC Opinion and an
Advice Letter. You should consult with your City Attorney
regarding these. In the future, you may wish to request our
advice as to a particular, upcoming decision before participating
in that decision.

Roy R. Sissell
May 12, 1981
Page Three


Lastly, I note from your ad in the "Yellow Pages" that
you advertise "City Violations Corrected." It is not clear
to me what this service entails, but there may be cause for
concern if you utilize your status as a City Councilman to
have City personnel sign off on corrections to violations
they have cited. In any event, you may wish to consult with your
City Attorney or with us in this regard. We would need more
information in order to advise you fully on this subject.

Sincerely,


Robert E. Leidigh
Counsel
Legal Division

REL:kp
Enc.: Thorner Opinion, 1 FPPC 198 (No. 75-089, 12/4/75)
Advice Letter to James Rodgers, May 24, 1979 (S18702)

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a city council member who voted to approve a HUD-funded low-income home rehabilitation project can subsequently bid as a plumbing subcontractor on work arising from that project, under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Political Reform Act does not prohibit bidding on the project itself; however, the council member should have disqualified themselves from the initial approval vote if they could reasonably foresee the project would generate $100 or more in income to their business. Additionally, caution is advised regarding using council status to facilitate violations corrections through city personnel.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document formatted as response letter to telephone advice request with factual background and legal analysis. No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections; extracted question and conclusion from body text. Strong confidence in extraction despite lack of standard section headers.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a city council member who voted to approve a HUD-funded low-income home rehabilitation project can subsequently bid as a plumbing subcontractor on work arising from that project, under the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: The Political Reform Act does not prohibit bidding on the project itself; however, the council member should have disqualified themselves from the initial approval vote if they could reasonably foresee the project would generate $100 or more in income to their business. Additionally, caution is advised regarding using council status to facilitate violations corrections through city personnel.

embedding.summary: FPPC counsel responds to a telephone advice request from Tracy City Council Member Roy Sissell regarding whether he may bid on plumbing subcontract work arising from a HUD project he voted to approve, explaining that while the bidding itself is permitted, disqualification should have occurred if increased income was foreseeable at the time of the approval vote.
